retrieved_docs.title: Electrical Alternans -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the history and physical exam of a patient with electrical alternans. Describe the electrocardiogram of a patient with electrical alternans. Describe the etiologies associated with electrical alternans. Outline the importance of enhancing care coordination among the interprofessional team to help patients with electrical alternans achieve positive outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrical Alternans -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Electrical alternans is the alternating voltage noted in the QRS complexes seen on an ECG; refer to the image below. Electrical alternans can be diagnosed by the varying changes in the amplitude of the QRS complex. Of note, T-wave alternans can be present as well. This review focuses on the QRS complex alternans. These changes are thought to be caused by the anterior-posterior swinging of the heart with each contraction in the fluid-filled pericardial sac. As the heart swings, the electrical signals detected by the electrodes travel over a greater distance through the fluid resulting in reduced voltage at the electrodes. Alternans may also be seen in other physiology including myocardial disease, pneumothorax or emphysema wherein the electrical conductivity of the tissues between the heart and the electrodes are affected causing variations in the QRS amplitudes. Other causes of electrical alternans can include AV nodal reentrant tachycardia, bundle branch block, Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome (WOW), late premature ventricular complexes where the underlying change in conduction pathway is responsible for the change in the QRS amplitude observed on the ECG. In some ventricular tachycardias, changes in ion concentrations around the myocardium can result in regional changes in potassium causing varied action potential instability creating an alternans type pattern as different regions of the heart depolarize at varied levels of excitation. [5] It has been shown that deep breathing may be a cause of alternans in the precordial leads caused by the additional distance the electrical signal travels at full inhalation where electrical signals are reduced at the electrodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrical Alternans -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Electrical alternans, although considered pathognomonic for pericardial effusion, is not a diagnostic factor for effusion and cardiac tamponade as other pathologies can result in this ECG finding. When present on an ECG, early evaluation of the patient’s vitals and overall presentation are imperative. If and when the patient is stable following resuscitation, further evaluation of the heart, including echocardiogram, is necessary to identify the underlying pathology and rule out tamponade. If tamponade physiology is present on imaging or hemodynamic instability from tachycardic rhythms is present further action needs to be taken with a focus on hemodynamic stability, then correction of the underlying pathophysiology.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrical Alternans -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Electrical alternans is most commonly attributed to pericardial effusion and tamponade pathology. In this scenario, the classic ECG rhythm is due to the pendulum-like motion and rotation of the heart from beat to beat manifesting as varied amplitudes when detected by the ECG electrodes. Electrical alternans is also seen in conduction and refractory changes in various rhythms including those mentioned above resulting in varied QRS amplitudes as detected by the electrodes. The mechanism that leads to these ECG changes will be further discussed in pathophysiology. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrical Alternans -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Electrical alternans is defined as alternating QRS amplitudes in any or all leads on an electrocardiogram (ECG) with no additional evident changes in conduction pathways of the heart. This rhythm is typically associated with pericardial effusion from fluid surrounding the heart. However, electrical alternans is also associated with other pathologies including but not limited to ventricular tachycardia, Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW), accelerated idioventricular rhythm, and supraventricular tachycardia. Recognizing the varied pathologies causing this ECG finding is key to evaluation and if necessary, successful management. This activity describes the evaluation, diagnosis, and management of electrical alternans and stresses the role of team-based interprofessional care for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrical Alternans -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare workers should be aware that Electrical alternans, although considered pathognomonic for pericardial effusion, is not a diagnostic factor for effusion and cardiac tamponade as other pathologies can result in this ECG finding. If the rhythm is noted on the ECG, the next step is to order an echocardiogram and consult with a cardiologist. If the patient is hemodynamically unstable, immediate drainage of the pericardial fluid is required. The most compelling fact is that electrical alternans is not a benign event and one should always rule out cardiac tamponade first.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrical Alternans -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: When electrical alternans is present on an ECG, further evaluation is needed to find the underlying cause as mentioned above. When considering a pericardial effusion, a transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) is the first-line imaging modality. In the unstable patient, bedside echocardiogram is even more important as TTE provides very high sensitivity (near 100%) in diagnosing pericardial effusion. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Pulsus Alternans -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Timing of events and the series of occurrences are important details one should pay attention to. Rarely, there can be a discrepancy between the electrical and mechanical activities of the heart, and echocardiography is an important tool to identify such inconsistency. Furthermore, there may be a discrepancy between the efficiency of the heart and the mechanical activation of the heart, the production of cardiac output, and in such cases, echocardiography can play a crucial role in diagnosing such a condition. Combining information from 2D and tissue Doppler evaluation of contractility, ECG tracing, and spectral tracing across the left ventricular outflow tract can provide an assessment of the mechanical activation of the heart, electrical activation of the heart muscle, and the resulting “success” of these efforts in producing cardiac output.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrical Alternans -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: When electrical alternans is found on an ECG, further evaluation is needed because treatment will vary based on the underlying pathophysiology. Therefore, a thorough history and examination of the patient are necessary to focus on the cardiovascular and pulmonary exam. In the case of pericardial effusion, patients are usually asymptomatic unless tamponade physiology is present. Signs of tamponade include Becks triad: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and distended neck veins. Additional signs of tamponade may also include tachypnea and dyspnea in the form of Kussmaul breathing and Pulsus Paradoxus. [6] In the case of arrhythmias, as mentioned above, patients may present with tachycardia and associated symptoms of light-headedness, fatigue, chest pain, shortness of breath, syncope, among others along with a potential history of cardiac conduction abnormalities. Based on this, a further cardiac history, including family history, may need to be obtained.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrical Alternans -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Electrical alternans is usually associated with pericardial effusion which is associated with many disease states including infection, inflammatory syndromes, trauma, malignancy, and idiopathic to name a few. Although the majority of effusions have an underlying cause, as much as 20% are from unknown causes, making approach and eventual treatment challenging. Incidence of pericardial effusion overall is low in symptomatic patients presenting with ECG findings of alternans. Despite this, effusion should remain on the differential as this is a potentially fatal complication if it remains unrecognized. [4] As mentioned above, there are additional pathologies that can lead to alternans on ECG also includes various arrhythmias and conduction pathway abnormalities.  Each of these pathologies have various and unique pathologies and further discussion of these will not be included in this paper.

retrieved_docs.title: Pulsus Alternans -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Whenever pulsus alternans is suspected, the appropriate cardiac workup should be completed, including laboratory work, chest X-ray, electrocardiogram, and echocardiogram. Objective laboratory findings include elevated pro-BNP and pulmonary vascular congestion on chest X-ray. Electrocardiogram will often show normal sinus rhythm or sinus tachycardia. [2] [13] [14] Notably, there will typically be no electrical alternans on the electrocardiogram, as this is found with electrical alternans and not pulsus alternans. [15] An echocardiogram will show alternating beat-to-beat arterial waveforms, the pathognomonic finding for pulsus alternans. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrical Alternans -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Electrical alternans is defined as alternating QRS amplitude that is seen in any or all leads on an electrocardiogram (ECG) with no additional changes to the conduction pathways of the heart. This rhythm is typically associated with pericardial effusion via the “swinging heart” from the fluid surrounding the heart. However, electrical alternans is also associated with other pathologies including but not limited to ventricular tachycardia, Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW), accelerated idioventricular rhythm, and supraventricular tachycardia. [1] Understanding the varied pathologies causing this ECG finding is key to evaluation and if necessary, treatment success.

retrieved_docs.title: Ashman Phenomenon -- Evaluation -- Electrocardiography Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Relatively long cycle immediately preceding the cycle terminated by the aberrant QRS complex A consistent pattern of a relatively long preceding R-R cycle immediately ahead of the short R-R cycle results in a wide QRS complex.

retrieved_docs.title: Ashman Phenomenon -- Evaluation -- Electrocardiography Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A short-long-short interval is even more likely to initiate aberration Finding an example of a shorter-longer-shorter R-R cycle pattern with less QRS width is contrary to the Ashman phenomenon, and that would argue for other causes of at least some of the wide complexes.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrical Alternans -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Alternans bundle branch block Aortic stenosis Air embolism Bigeminy Cardiac rupture Digoxin toxicity- bidirectional ventricular tachycardia Myocardial contusion Massive pulmonary embolus Wide varying tidal volumes in severe tachypnea Ventricular septal defect

retrieved_docs.title: Dressler Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An electrocardiograph (ECG) in a patient with Dressler syndrome will initially demonstrate global ST-segment elevation and T-wave inversion, such as with pericarditis. Further inflammation of the myocardium will also result in ST-segment elevations. Both electrical alternans (variation in amplitude or directionality of QRS from beat to beat) and/or a low voltage QRS may also be observed if there is a large volume of pericardial effusion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2553
retrieved_docs.content: ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia are directly related to اضطراب التوصيل (conduction disorder), and the ECG finding described as non‑sinus rhythm has a specific focus on اضطراب التوصيل. Both the use of ECG monitors for arrhythmia detection and the identification of non‑sinus rhythms on an ECG therefore pertain to recognizing and evaluating conduction disturbances, which is essential for accurate diagnosis and management of patients with arrhythmic and conduction‑related cardiac conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of ventricular tachycardia target the intrinsic accelerated ventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل) and its subtype ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني). Understanding these interconnected entities—electrical conduction abnormalities, structural cardiac changes, specific arrhythmic syndromes, diagnostic measurements, and therapeutic interventions—is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management of patients with cardiac rhythm disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: type of electrocardiogram, while the broader term electrocardiogram (تخطيط كهربية القلب) encompasses related concepts such as electrographic cardiac monitoring and ECG rhythm‑strip transmission via telephonic or telemetric channels, which is focused on remote heart measurement (قياس القلب عن بعد). ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia are directly related to electrocardiographic monitoring and are linked to conduction disorders (اضطراب التوصيل). The entity ECG: non‑sinus rhythm is also a subtype of electrocardiographic monitoring, with a particular focus on conduction disorders. Both ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia and ECG: non‑sinus rhythm therefore represent specific monitoring modalities used to detect and assess arrhythmic and conduction abnormalities. The term electrocardiogram is classified as تخطيط كهربية القلب and maintains a broader relationship with the concept ECG, reinforcing its role as the foundational diagnostic tool from which the various monitoring techniques, including electrographic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 183
retrieved_docs.content: acceleration (التسارع البطيني), which itself is a subtype of an accelerated idioventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل). Together, these concepts illustrate how electrocardiographic monitoring and cardiac electrophysiology studies are interrelated tools for diagnosing conduction abnormalities, assessing arrhythmic risk, guiding therapeutic ablation, and supporting cardiac rehabilitation, thereby informing comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1783
retrieved_docs.content: Electrographic cardiac monitoring, referred to in Arabic as تخطيط كهربية القلب ECG, is a diagnostic technique that records the electrical activity of the heart to assess cardiac rhythm and function. This monitoring method provides clinicians with real‑time information about cardiac electrophysiology, aiding in the detection of arrhythmias, myocardial ischemia, and other cardiac abnormalities. By establishing a direct relationship between the monitoring process and the ECG trace, healthcare providers can evaluate heart health, guide treatment decisions, and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 183
retrieved_docs.content: recording. The entity ECG: non‑sinus rhythm is a subtype of electrocardiographic monitoring and its primary focus is on conduction disorders (اضطراب التوصيل). Likewise, ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia has a relationship with conduction disorders, highlighting the role of ECG devices in detecting and characterizing abnormal cardiac conduction. Cardiac electrophysiologic study (دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية) is defined as the invasive evaluation that includes arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic‑focus ablation, and treatment of specific tachyarrhythmias. This study is applied to atrial fibrillation, supraventricular tachycardia, and ventricular tachycardia, each being an instance of the electrophysiologic evaluation. The ventricular‑tachycardia evaluation specifically focuses on ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني), which itself is a subtype of an accelerated idioventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل). Together, these concepts illustrate how electrocardiographic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Heart rate and rhythm disorders are classified as a subset of conduction disorders, indicating that these arrhythmic conditions arise from abnormalities in the cardiac electrical conduction system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 924
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac electrophysiology study (دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية) is closely linked to conduction disorder (اضطراب التوصيل). Electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of atrial fibrillation, as well as the analogous evaluations for supraventricular tachycardia and ventricular tachycardia, are each classified as types of cardiac electrophysiology study. ECG monitors for arrhythmia (ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia) and ECG recordings that show a non‑sinus rhythm (ECG: non-sinus rhythm) both have a direct relationship with, and focus on, conduction disorder. Ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني) is a subcategory of an intrinsic accelerated ventricular system (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل) and serves as the primary focus of the electrophysiologic evaluation and ablation treatment for ventricular tachycardia. Together, these concepts illustrate how electrophysiologic studies, ECG monitoring, and specific arrhythmic mechanisms are interrelated in the assessment and management of cardiac conduction abnormalities and ventricular acceleration disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1054
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac electrophysiology study (دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية) serves as the overarching diagnostic and therapeutic framework for evaluating and treating cardiac arrhythmias. Within this framework, electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of atrial fibrillation is classified as a type of cardiac electrophysiology study, as is electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of ventricular tachycardia. The ventricular‑tachycardia evaluation specifically has a focus on ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني), which itself is a component of an intrinsic accelerated cardiac system (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل). Both the atrial‑fibrillation and ventricular‑tachycardia procedures are closely linked to conduction disorders (اضطراب التوصيل), reflecting the central role of abnormal electrical conduction in these arrhythmias. ECG monitors that detect arrhythmia and ECG recordings showing non‑sinus rhythm also have a direct relationship with conduction disorders, underscoring the importance of surface electrocardiographic monitoring in identifying and guiding treatment of these electrophysiologic abnormalities. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate how detailed electrophysiologic evaluation, targeted ablation, and comprehensive ECG monitoring converge to diagnose, characterize, and manage conduction disturbances and accelerated ventricular activity in patients with complex cardiac arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 985
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic monitoring is a comprehensive approach to evaluating cardiovascular function that may include tilt‑table testing, continuous electrocardiogram (ECG) recording, intermittent blood‑pressure monitoring, and optional pharmacologic intervention. It also serves as the basis for physician‑provided outpatient cardiac‑rehabilitation services that incorporate continuous ECG monitoring during each session. Within this broad category, specific diagnostic and prognostic techniques are considered subtypes: GSPECT of the myocardium using technetium‑99m sestamibi provides myocardial perfusion imaging, and measurement of microvolt T‑wave alternans assesses ventricular arrhythmia risk. The entity “ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia” is directly related to electrocardiographic monitoring and is specifically associated with conduction disorders (اضطراب التوصيل). Likewise, “ECG: non‑sinus rhythm” is classified as a form of electrocardiographic monitoring and its primary focus is also on conduction disorders. Together, these interconnected concepts illustrate how continuous ECG‑based monitoring, specialized imaging, and electrophysiological assessments are integrated to detect, evaluate, and manage arrhythmias and conduction abnormalities, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care in cardiology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: كهربية القلب and maintains a broader relationship with the concept ECG, reinforcing its role as the foundational diagnostic tool from which the various monitoring techniques, including electrographic cardiac monitoring and remote rhythm‑strip transmission, derive. This integrated framework highlights how electrocardiographic monitoring and its related sub‑modalities are interconnected within cardiac diagnostic and rehabilitation practice, supporting accurate detection of arrhythmias, assessment of ventricular risk, and remote patient monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2765
retrieved_docs.content: CARDIAC CONDUCTION DISEASE WITH OR WITHOUT DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY is known to have cardiac dysrhythmias as a clinical manifestation. Cardiac dysrhythmias represent a broad spectrum of abnormal heart rhythm disorders, and within this spectrum the specific arrhythmia supraventricular tachycardia (تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ) is classified. Understanding that supraventricular tachycardia is a type of cardiac dysrhythmia clarifies the hierarchical relationship among these concepts and aids clinicians in diagnosing and managing patients who present with conduction disease, possible dilated cardiomyopathy, and associated arrhythmic events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1138
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic monitoring is a broad clinical domain that includes continuous ECG observation during outpatient cardiac rehabilitation performed by physicians or other qualified health‑care professionals, as well as advanced imaging and electrophysiologic assessments such as GSPECT of the myocardium using technetium‑99m sestamibi, measurement of microvolt T‑wave alternans for ventricular arrhythmia risk stratification, and stimulation of the carotid sinus with simultaneous ECG recording. Within this domain, ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia are specifically linked to electrocardiographic monitoring and are associated with conduction disorders (اضطراب التوصيل). The entity ECG: non‑sinus rhythm is classified as a type of electrocardiographic monitoring and also has a focus on conduction disorders, underscoring its relevance to the detection and evaluation of abnormal cardiac rhythm patterns. Together, these interconnected concepts illustrate how various ECG‑based techniques and related procedures are employed to diagnose, monitor, and manage arrhythmias and conduction abnormalities, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care in cardiology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 5 -- Community 2946
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy), indicating that structural changes in the left ventricle often accompany abnormal heart rhythms. In addition, cardiac dysrhythmias frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية (atrial flutter), reflecting a common overlap of rhythm disturbances that can influence diagnosis, management, and prognosis in patients with cardiac electrical abnormalities. Recognizing the link between cardiac dysrhythmias, left ventricular hypertrophy, and atrial flutter is important for comprehensive cardiac assessment and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2636
retrieved_docs.content: The electrocardiogram, also referred to in Arabic as تخطيط كهربية القلب, is classified as an electrocardiogram rhythm‑strip telephonic or telemetric transmission. This form of transmission is specifically focused on قياس القلب عن بعد, meaning remote measurement of the heart. By delivering rhythm‑strip data through telephone or telemetry channels, the electrocardiogram enables clinicians to assess cardiac electrical activity and heart function from a distance, supporting remote cardiac monitoring and diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: structural heart changes, electrophysiologic abnormalities, diagnostic terminologies, monitoring technologies, interventional procedures, pharmacologic considerations, and systemic organ interactions essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and holistic cardiac patient care.
